After extensive litigation over religious exemptions, the Trump administration issued rules broadly exempting religious and moral objectors. Pennsylvania and New Jersey challenged the new exemptions as beyond agency authority.
doctrine_prediction: A principled executive-restraint and agency-deference adherent would hold that the agencies must act within the statutory authority Congress granted; expanding exemptions beyond what the statute clearly authorizes would exceed delegated authority. At the same time, a principled deference adherent would accept a reasonable agency construction of its own mandate's scope, including the decision to exempt religious objectors if Congress's silence on exemptions is ambiguous.
r_party_prediction: The Republican Party and Trump administration strongly supported the expanded religious exemptions as aligned with both religious liberty goals and the administration's policy of reducing ACA requirements.
d_party_prediction: The Democratic Party opposed the expanded exemptions as gutting the contraceptive mandate for millions of women employees and argued the agencies exceeded their statutory authority.
signal_explanation: The 7-2 majority included Kagan and Breyer concurring in judgment (not joining the majority's broader reasoning), suggesting mixed signals. The case is diagnostic primarily because D-appointed justices Kagan and Breyer concurred in upholding a Trump administration rule against D party interest, though they limited their agreement. R-appointed justices' votes are non-diagnostic (both party and deference doctrine support the rule if agencies had authority).

title: Collins v. Yellen

title: Free Enterprise Fund v. PCAOB
description: The Court held 5-4 that dual for-cause removal protections insulating PCAOB members from presidential control were unconstitutional. The President must be able to hold principal officers accountable by removing inferior officers beneath them without a second layer of tenure protection.
factual_background: The Sarbanes-Oxley Act created the Public Company Accounting Oversight Board (PCAOB) whose members could be removed by the SEC only for cause, and SEC commissioners were themselves removable by the President only for cause — creating two insulating layers of protection from presidential removal.
doctrine_prediction: A principled adherent of executive restraint holds that the Take Care Clause requires the President to maintain meaningful control over subordinate executive officers. Two stacked layers of for-cause removal protection impermissibly insulate inferior officers from the President and are unconstitutional regardless of Congress's intent to create an independent expert body.

d_party_prediction: Democrats supported the PCAOB's insulated structure as a necessary design feature of an independent expert body charged with overseeing securities markets, consistent with Congress's authority to structure executive agencies.

Sotomayor's dissent aligns with D party interest in agency independence but also with a plausible constitutional reading.

title: Zivotofsky v. Kerry
description: The Court held 6-3 that the President possesses exclusive constitutional authority to recognize foreign nations and governments, and that a federal statute requiring State Department to list 'Israel' as birthplace for Jerusalem-born citizens impermissibly encroached on that power.
factual_background: Congress passed a law in 2002 allowing Americans born in Jerusalem to have 'Israel' listed as their birthplace on passports. President Bush signed the bill but issued a signing statement asserting the provision interfered with the President's recognition authority. The Obama administration refused to apply the provision. Menachem Zivotofsky's parents sued for compliance.
doctrine_prediction: A principled adherent of executive restraint holds that executive power must be grounded in specific constitutional text. The Constitution's grant to the President of power to 'receive Ambassadors' textually embeds exclusive recognition authority in Article II; congressional statutes that purport to override that textual grant exceed Congress's authority under the Presentment Clause and impermissibly direct the content of diplomatic recognition.
r_party_prediction: Republicans in Congress passed the 2002 law and politically supported listing 'Israel' as the birthplace for Jerusalem-born citizens; they sought to use legislation to bind presidential recognition policy, effectively restraining executive discretion on this sensitive diplomatic question in favor of a congressional preference.
d_party_prediction: The Obama administration (and the Democratic Party's position) held that presidential recognition authority is exclusive under Article II and cannot be overridden by statute, defending broad executive power in the foreign-affairs domain.
signal_explanation: The case produces a cross-cutting signal: R-appointed dissenters (Roberts, Alito) voted to uphold the congressional law their party passed, aligning with party but against the doctrine's logic of exclusive executive recognition authority. D-appointed justices and Thomas concurred in striking down the law, aligning with doctrine but splitting from typical partisan expectations about executive power.

title: Department of Commerce v. New York

Evidence emerged that the rationale was post-hoc: Ross had decided to add the question before the VRA justification was constructed. Lower courts blocked the question.

title: Gundy v. United States
description: A 4-1-4 decision in which a four-Justice plurality upheld a broad delegation to the Attorney General in SORNA, while three dissenters urged adoption of a stricter nondelegation standard. Alito concurred in the judgment but signaled openness to revisiting the intelligible-principle test if a majority formed. The case previewed the emerging nondelegation debate.
factual_background: The Sex Offender Registration and Notification Act (SORNA) required sex offenders convicted before the law's enactment to register, but delegated to the Attorney General authority to decide whether and how SORNA applied to pre-enactment offenders. Herman Gundy challenged this delegation as an unconstitutional grant of legislative power to the executive.
doctrine_prediction: A principled adherent of executive restraint holds that Congress may not hand core legislative decisions to the executive without providing a meaningful standard to guide discretion. SORNA's delegation — giving the Attorney General essentially unchecked discretion over a major class of persons — lacks an intelligible principle and allows the executive to exercise quasi-legislative power in violation of Article I's vesting of all legislative power in Congress.
r_party_prediction: Republicans and the Trump administration supported upholding SORNA's registration requirements but ideologically supported stricter nondelegation limits on agency power generally; the Republican Party platforms in this era called for limiting the administrative state.
d_party_prediction: Democrats supported broad congressional delegations to executive agencies as necessary for effective governance; the D-party position was that the intelligible-principle test was adequate and that a stricter nondelegation doctrine would disrupt decades of regulatory programs.
signal_explanation: The 4-4-1 split with Alito's notable concurrence signaling interest in a stricter nondelegation standard provides strong evidence of partisan-doctrinal tension. Sotomayor and Kagan in the plurality voted against the doctrine's demand for meaningful limits, aligning with D-party preference for broad delegations. Gorsuch, Roberts, and Thomas in dissent aligned both with doctrine and R-party anti-administrative-state rhetoric.

title: United States v. Arthrex, Inc.
description: The Court held 5-4 that Patent Trial and Appeal Board Administrative Patent Judges (APJs), as constituted, exercised the authority of principal officers but were appointed as inferior officers — violating the Appointments Clause. Roberts's plurality remedied this by requiring that the Director of the USPTO could review APJ decisions.
factual_background: Arthrex challenged an inter partes review proceeding before USPTO's Patent Trial and Appeal Board, arguing that the APJs who decided the case were unconstitutionally appointed inferior officers exercising principal-officer authority because their decisions were final and unreviewable by the Director.
doctrine_prediction: A principled adherent of executive restraint holds that the Appointments Clause requires that any officer exercising substantial, final authority over third parties be either presidentially appointed or subject to supervision by a principal officer who is. Structuring an agency so that inferior officers issue unreviewable final orders removes them from the constitutional chain of accountability the Appointments Clause was designed to preserve.
r_party_prediction: The Trump administration and Republicans supported structural reforms requiring greater executive oversight of patent adjudicators, consistent with the broader R-party goal of subjecting agency adjudication to presidential accountability chains.
d_party_prediction: Democrats and patent bar stakeholders generally supported the existing PTAB structure as an expert tribunal whose independence from political direction was a feature; the D-party position favored maintaining the insulated adjudicative design.
signal_explanation: The 5-4 split follows the partisan appointment pattern, but the case involves specialized patent procedure rather than a high-salience political issue, moderating the diagnostic signal. Sotomayor and Kagan dissented on the remedy, not the constitutional holding, further complicating a clean partisan read.

Breyer's dissent (not in seat map) argued that the majority's approach would politically contaminate an expert technical tribunal — a principled institutional argument not reducible to partisanship.
The 5-4 split on remedy rather than constitutional holding means the partisan alignment is less clean than the overall vote count suggests.

title: Utility Air Regulatory Group v. EPA
description: The Court held 5-4 that the EPA could not rewrite the Clean Air Act's numerical thresholds to regulate greenhouse gases from stationary sources under the PSD and Title V programs. While the agency had authority to regulate GHGs from mobile sources, it could not apply the same trigger thresholds to stationary sources without exceeding the statutory text.
factual_background: After Massachusetts v. EPA (2007) held that greenhouse gases were 'air pollutants' under the Clean Air Act, the EPA extended GHG regulation to large stationary sources under Prevention of Significant Deterioration (PSD) and Title V programs. Industry groups argued the EPA impermissibly rewrote the statutory thresholds — which were set for conventional pollutants — to make them workable for GHGs.
doctrine_prediction: A principled adherent of agency-deference doctrine holds that agencies must stay within the textual limits Congress enacted and cannot substitute their own numbers for those Congress chose. Where a statute sets specific numerical thresholds, an agency that rewrites those numbers to make its preferred policy workable exceeds its statutory authority, regardless of how reasonable the policy goal is.
r_party_prediction: Republicans and industry groups supported limiting the EPA's GHG regulation, arguing the agency had exceeded its statutory authority and that major new climate regulations required explicit congressional authorization rather than agency initiative.
d_party_prediction: The Obama administration and Democrats defended the EPA's approach as a reasonable interpretation of broad statutory language to address the urgent problem of climate change, arguing courts should defer to the agency's technical judgment about how to apply the Act.
signal_explanation: The 5-4 split follows partisan appointment lines precisely, with all R-appointed justices in the majority limiting EPA authority and all D-appointed justices dissenting. This clean partisan alignment on a significant climate regulation question provides a strong diagnostic signal for both partisanship and doctrine.

seat_1.vote: Joined the majority limiting EPA's authority to rewrite statutory thresholds; would have gone further to deny EPA authority to regulate GHGs from stationary sources entirely

Thomas and the three joining R-appointed justices are all coded non-diagnostic (doctrine-yes/party-yes) because they aligned with both doctrine and party on limiting EPA.
The case predates Chevron's eventual overruling in Loper Bright; the majority used a Chevron step-one analysis to find the rewrite impermissible.

Scalia's majority opinion acknowledged EPA's GHG regulation of mobile sources was valid under Massachusetts v. EPA, limiting the scope of the anti-deference holding to the threshold-rewriting question.
D-appointed justices' votes to uphold EPA could be read as aligned with a different doctrinal tradition — Chevron deference — rather than as purely partisan.

title: Michigan v. EPA
description: The Court held 5-4 that the EPA acted unreasonably by failing to consider costs when deciding whether it was 'appropriate and necessary' to regulate mercury emissions from power plants under the MATS rule. Agencies must consider all relevant factors — including economic cost — unless Congress clearly forbids it.
factual_background: The EPA's Mercury and Air Toxics Standards (MATS) rule regulated hazardous air pollutant emissions from power plants. The EPA concluded that regulation was 'appropriate and necessary' without considering compliance costs at the threshold finding stage, later claiming costs would be considered in setting specific emission standards. The compliance cost was estimated at $9.6 billion annually against monetized benefits of $4-6 million directly attributable to the targeted pollutants.
doctrine_prediction: A principled adherent of agency-deference doctrine holds that an agency must consider all relevant factors — and cost is nearly always relevant — before exercising major regulatory authority. Refusing to consider whether the cost of a regulatory program is justified at the threshold decision is not a permissible interpretation of a statute that requires a regulation to be 'appropriate' — a term that inherently demands a balancing judgment.
r_party_prediction: Republicans and states challenged the MATS rule as regulatory overreach, arguing the EPA ignored enormous compliance costs in pursuit of its climate and air quality agenda, and that Congress did not authorize regulations that impose billions in costs for minimal direct benefits.
d_party_prediction: The Obama EPA and Democrats defended the MATS rule as a necessary protection against hazardous air pollutants from the nation's dirtiest power plants, arguing that co-benefits (reduction of particulates) justified the costs and that the statute did not require cost consideration at the threshold stage.
signal_explanation: The 5-4 split follows partisan appointment lines, but the legal question — whether 'appropriate and necessary' requires cost consideration — is a genuine statutory interpretation question rather than a clear case of agency overreach. The moderate signal reflects real doctrinal ambiguity about what 'appropriate' requires.

On remand, the DC Circuit found EPA had adequately justified the rule on cost grounds, so the practical effect of the majority's ruling was limited.

Kagan's dissent argued 'appropriate and necessary' naturally means appropriate from a public-health standpoint, making the statutory interpretation question genuinely close rather than a clear case of agency overreach.

title: Kisor v. Wilkie
description: The Court declined 5-4 to overrule Auer deference (requiring courts to defer to an agency's reasonable interpretation of its own ambiguous regulations), but significantly narrowed its scope. Kagan wrote the majority narrowing Auer; Gorsuch wrote the principal dissent urging complete abandonment of Auer deference.
factual_background: James Kisor, a Vietnam veteran, sought to reopen his disability benefits claim based on newly discovered service records. The Board of Veterans Appeals denied reopening, deferring to the VA's interpretation of its own regulation on what constituted 'relevant' evidence. Kisor argued courts should not defer to agencies' self-serving interpretations of their own regulations.
doctrine_prediction: A principled adherent of agency-deference doctrine holds that judicial deference to agency interpretations of ambiguous statutes and regulations must be grounded in congressional delegation of interpretive authority to the agency, not in agency expertise alone. Where an agency's regulation is genuinely ambiguous and the agency's interpretation falls within the range of reasonable meanings, courts may credit that interpretation — but only after the court independently determines the regulation is truly ambiguous and the agency's reading is not a post-hoc litigation position.
r_party_prediction: Republicans and conservative legal groups urged the Court to overrule Auer entirely, arguing that deference to agencies' self-serving interpretation of their own regulations violated separation of powers and gave agencies incentive to write vague regulations to expand their own power.
d_party_prediction: Democrats and the Trump administration (unusually aligned) both sought to preserve some form of deference to agency interpretations, though the Trump administration's position on Auer was somewhat equivocal; the D-party position was that agency expertise and institutional competence justified deference to agency regulatory interpretations.
signal_explanation: The 5-4 split with Gorsuch's powerful dissent calling for Auer's overruling provides a strong signal of partisan-doctrinal division on the foundational question of administrative law. Sotomayor and Kagan in the majority voted to preserve deference, aligning with D-party interest in agency authority. Thomas, Roberts, and Alito in the 'against Auer' bloc aligned with R-party anti-administrative-state positions.

Auer deference was ultimately overruled by Loper Bright in 2024, vindicating the dissent's analytical framework.

Kagan's majority can be read as a genuine middle-ground compromise that actually strengthened judicial review by adding preconditions to deference, making her vote more doctrinal than partisan.

Roberts and Kavanaugh's concurrences show internal R-bloc fragmentation: they declined to join both the majority and the dissent, suggesting the partisan signal is less clean than the 5-4 tally implies.

title: Alabama Assoc. of Realtors v. HHS
description: The Court held 6-3 per curiam that the CDC likely lacked statutory authority under Section 361 of the Public Health Service Act to impose a nationwide eviction moratorium, applying a nascent major-questions principle. The Court vacated the stay of the lower court's injunction blocking the moratorium.
factual_background: The CDC issued a nationwide eviction moratorium during the COVID-19 pandemic, citing Section 361 of the Public Health Service Act, which authorizes measures 'necessary to prevent the spread of communicable disease.' When that authority was set to expire, Congress did not extend it; the Biden administration allowed the CDC to re-issue the moratorium unilaterally. Landlords challenged the CDC's authority.
doctrine_prediction: A principled adherent of agency-deference doctrine holds that when an agency claims authority to regulate a vast economic sector through a vague catchall provision in a public-health statute — rather than through a specific statutory grant — courts should not defer to the agency's expansive interpretation. Congress speaks clearly when it authorizes major economic interventions; statutory silence or generality does not license agencies to resolve major economic questions on their own.
r_party_prediction: Republicans and landlord groups strongly opposed the CDC eviction moratorium as an unconstitutional assertion of executive power that lacked clear congressional authorization and exceeded the agency's statutory mandate under a public health statute.
d_party_prediction: The Biden administration and Democrats defended the moratorium as within CDC's broad statutory authority to take measures necessary to prevent disease spread, arguing the pandemic emergency justified an expansive reading of the agency's public-health powers.
signal_explanation: The 6-3 vote was per curiam with only Breyer, Sotomayor, and Kagan noted as dissenters. The conservative majority applied the major-questions principle without full briefing or argument. The signal is moderate because the decision was on an emergency stay application rather than full merits, and the legal question (CDC's statutory authority) was genuinely novel.

title: Ohio v. EPA
description: The Court granted a stay of EPA's Good Neighbor Plan 5-4, holding that EPA likely acted arbitrarily and capriciously by failing to adequately respond to states' objections to their state implementation plan disapprovals before promulgating the federal plan. Gorsuch wrote for the majority; Roberts, Sotomayor, Kagan, and Jackson dissented.
factual_background: EPA's Good Neighbor Plan regulated upwind states' contributions to downwind air pollution under the Clean Air Act. When EPA disapproved numerous states' implementation plans, those states challenged the disapprovals; several courts stayed those disapprovals. EPA nonetheless finalized the federal Good Neighbor Plan, relying on the participation of states whose plans had been stayed. Multiple states and industry groups sought an emergency stay of the entire federal plan.
doctrine_prediction: A principled adherent of agency-deference doctrine holds that agency rules must rest on reasoned decision-making that responds to all material objections. When the legal and factual premises underlying a rule change dramatically mid-rulemaking — here, stays of state plan disapprovals — an agency that refuses to reconsider the rule's viability acts arbitrarily. Courts are not required to defer to an agency that ignores the logical foundation of its own regulatory scheme.
r_party_prediction: Republicans and Republican-led states strongly opposed the Good Neighbor Plan as federal overreach that imposed costly emissions controls on upwind states for the benefit of distant downwind jurisdictions, and supported staying the rule pending judicial review of EPA's disapproval decisions.
d_party_prediction: The Biden EPA and Democrats defended the Good Neighbor Plan as necessary to address interstate air pollution under the Act's interstate transport provisions, arguing the stays of individual state plans did not undermine the federal plan's legal basis or workability.
signal_explanation: Roberts's notable dissent from the conservative majority — arguing the Court was too eager to intervene before the lower courts had ruled — provides meaningful cross-partisan signal. The 5-4 split otherwise follows party lines, but Roberts's position complicates a purely partisan reading. The stay posture also moderates the signal.

title: SEC v. Jarkesy
description: The Court held 6-3 that the Seventh Amendment's jury trial right applies to SEC civil enforcement actions seeking civil penalties, and that Congress cannot route such suits through administrative proceedings to avoid Article III courts. Roberts wrote for the majority.
factual_background: The SEC brought an administrative enforcement action against hedge fund manager George Jarkesy, alleging securities fraud. The Dodd-Frank Act allowed the SEC to choose between bringing enforcement actions in federal district court (with jury trial) or before an in-house ALJ. Jarkesy argued the SEC's use of an in-house tribunal to impose civil penalties violated his Seventh Amendment right to a jury trial.
doctrine_prediction: A principled adherent of agency-deference doctrine holds that Congress may not abrogate constitutional rights — including the Seventh Amendment jury trial right for common-law-like claims — simply by routing them through administrative agencies. Agency adjudication of civil penalties that closely resemble common-law fraud actions must comport with the Seventh Amendment; courts should not defer to Congress's or agencies' choices about tribunal design when constitutional rights are at stake.
r_party_prediction: Republicans and business groups supported limiting in-house agency enforcement proceedings, viewing the SEC's ALJ system as rigged in favor of the agency, and supported requiring jury trials in federal court for civil penalties.
d_party_prediction: Democrats and the SEC defended the ALJ system as a congressional choice to use expert tribunals for complex securities enforcement, arguing the public-rights exception permitted Congress to route enforcement actions through administrative proceedings without triggering the Seventh Amendment.
signal_explanation: The 6-3 split precisely follows the partisan appointment divide, with the six Republican-appointed justices in the majority limiting SEC's enforcement machinery and the three Democratic-appointed justices dissenting. The case represents a significant structural limitation on the administrative state, making it highly diagnostic.

The 'public rights' exception to the Seventh Amendment is a genuinely contested area of constitutional law where reasonable jurists disagree, meaning the 6-3 split may reflect jurisprudential rather than partisan views.

title: Relentless, Inc. v. Department of Commerce
description: Decided as a companion to Loper Bright, the Court held 6-3 that NMFS's herring-monitor cost rule exceeded its statutory authority. More broadly, the Court's decision in Loper Bright (overruling Chevron) governs: courts must exercise independent judgment in statutory interpretation rather than deferring to agency readings of ambiguous statutes.
factual_background: Relentless Inc. challenged a National Marine Fisheries Service rule requiring fishing vessels to pay the costs of federal observers who monitored compliance with fishery management plans. As in Loper Bright, the question was whether NMFS had statutory authority to require industry-funded monitoring. Jackson, who participated in Relentless but was recused from Loper Bright (having served on the panel below), dissented.
doctrine_prediction: A principled adherent of agency-deference doctrine holds that courts must read statutes independently and not defer to agencies' preferred interpretations of ambiguous provisions. Chevron deference improperly allowed agencies to exploit statutory ambiguity to expand their own authority; overruling Chevron restores courts' primary role as independent interpreters of law under Article III and the APA.
r_party_prediction: Republicans and conservative legal groups had long sought to overrule Chevron as a source of unlawful agency power expansion; overruling Chevron was a core goal of R-aligned legal organizations and the Federalist Society, and the Trump-era Court's composition made this possible.
d_party_prediction: Democrats and agency-side advocates defended Chevron as foundational to the modern administrative state, arguing that congressional delegations of ambiguity to agencies reflected deliberate legislative design and that overruling Chevron would destabilize vast areas of federal regulatory law.
signal_explanation: The 6-3 split in Relentless follows partisan appointment lines exactly, mirroring the Loper Bright decision. All six Republican-appointed justices voted to overrule Chevron; all three Democratic-appointed justices (including Jackson, who participated in Relentless) dissented. The overruling of a 40-year precedent on strict party lines is maximally diagnostic.

counterarguments: The overruling of Chevron could increase judicial discretion in regulatory cases, which may in practice benefit whichever party's legal coalition dominates the judiciary — the long-term partisan effects are uncertain.

Roberts's majority opinion preserved past Chevron decisions under stare decisis, significantly limiting the immediate practical disruption, which suggests the ruling was calibrated rather than maximally disruptive.

The three D-appointed dissenters' position that Chevron reflected congressional delegation is a legitimate statutory interpretation theory that does not require attributing their votes to partisan interest.

title: FCC v. Consumers' Research
description: The Court held 6-3 that the FCC's Universal Service Fund mechanism — which funds subsidized telecommunications access and is administered by a private entity (USAC) under FCC oversight — does not violate the nondelegation doctrine. Kavanaugh wrote for the majority; Gorsuch, Thomas, and Alito dissented.
factual_background: The Telecommunications Act of 1996 required telecommunications providers to contribute to a Universal Service Fund to subsidize access for low-income consumers and rural areas. The FCC delegated day-to-day administration of the fund to USAC, a private non-profit corporation subject to FCC direction. Challengers argued the delegation to a private entity and the FCC's rate-setting discretion violated the nondelegation doctrine.
doctrine_prediction: A principled adherent of agency-deference doctrine holds that Congress must provide meaningful guidance — an intelligible principle — when delegating legislative authority to executive agencies or private entities. A delegation that gives a private corporation unreviewable authority to set contribution rates affecting the entire telecommunications industry, without adequate governmental supervision, risks concentrating quasi-legislative power outside constitutional structures.
r_party_prediction: The Republican Party in 2025 was divided on this question: the Trump administration generally supported maintaining USF (a program benefiting rural and low-income users in R-leaning areas), while doctrinaire anti-administrative-state conservatives sought to apply the nondelegation doctrine to dismantle delegation-heavy regulatory programs.
d_party_prediction: Democrats broadly supported the USF as a vital program ensuring telecommunications access for underserved communities, and supported the FCC's authority to administer it through USAC; the D-party position was to uphold the delegation.
signal_explanation: The unusual 6-3 split — with three conservative justices (Gorsuch, Thomas, Alito) dissenting while three other conservatives (Roberts, Kavanaugh, Barrett) joined all three Democratic-appointed justices — fractures the expected partisan alignment. The dissenters' nondelegation position is doctrinally consistent but breaks from the R-party political interest in maintaining USF for rural constituents.

The three dissenting conservatives' nondelegation position is consistent with their votes in Gundy and other cases, suggesting a principled doctrinal stance rather than selective application.

The case is decided in 2025 and involves novel private-entity delegation questions where the doctrine has not crystallized, making it difficult to characterize any vote as clearly doctrine-compliant or non-compliant.

title: Clapper v. Amnesty International USA
description: The Court held 5-4 that attorneys and human rights organizations challenging FISA surveillance lacked standing because their alleged injury — that their communications with foreign clients might be intercepted — was too speculative to satisfy the injury-in-fact requirement.
factual_background: Section 702 of FISA Amendments Act of 2008 authorized the government to conduct surveillance of non-U.S. persons abroad. Attorneys, journalists, and organizations whose work required communicating with foreign individuals likely targeted for surveillance challenged the law's constitutionality. They argued they were chilled from communicating freely and had incurred costs protecting their communications.
doctrine_prediction: A principled adherent of standing doctrine holds that Article III requires a concrete, non-speculative injury in fact. Plaintiffs cannot manufacture standing by spending money to guard against speculative future harm or by self-censoring in anticipation of surveillance that may never occur. The injury-in-fact requirement must be taken seriously even when the underlying constitutional claim is weighty.
r_party_prediction: Republicans and the Obama administration jointly defended the constitutionality of the FISA surveillance program and urged the Court to deny standing, insulating a major national security tool from judicial challenge. R-party position supported broad government surveillance authority and strict standing limits that kept challenges out of court.
d_party_prediction: Civil liberties-oriented Democrats and the plaintiffs' coalition sought to establish standing to challenge mass surveillance, arguing the chilling effect on attorney-client and journalistic communications was a cognizable injury. The D-party institutional position was divided — the Obama administration defended the surveillance program while many D-aligned groups challenged it.
signal_explanation: The 5-4 split follows partisan appointment lines. Standing doctrine holds that speculative, self-manufactured injury should not satisfy Article III — and the R-appointed majority enforced this rigorously. D-appointed justices' willingness to find standing for speculative injuries aligns with D-party interest in enabling challenges to national-security surveillance but diverges from the doctrine's requirement of concrete harm.

Sotomayor and Kagan are coded party-partial because D-party institutional interest was itself divided: the executive branch (D) wanted standing denied; civil liberties groups (D-aligned) wanted standing granted.

Breyer's dissent (not in seat map) made a careful probabilistic argument that surveillance was sufficiently likely given the government's stated practices — a doctrinal argument rather than a partisan one.

The secrecy surrounding FISA surveillance made it structurally impossible for plaintiffs to prove concrete injury, which may mean the doctrine-enforcing majority was applying strict standing rules to deliberately insulate classified programs from review.

title: Spokeo, Inc. v. Robins
description: The Court held 6-2 that standing requires both particularized and concrete injury; a bare procedural violation of a federal statute does not automatically confer standing without a showing of real-world harm. The case was remanded to assess whether the statutory violation caused concrete injury to the plaintiff.
factual_background: Thomas Robins sued Spokeo — a 'people search engine' — under the Fair Credit Reporting Act, alleging Spokeo had published inaccurate information about him. The inaccuracies included a false zip code and inflated education and income levels. Robins argued the FCRA violations themselves constituted injury. The Ninth Circuit found standing based on violation of a statutory right; the Supreme Court vacated and remanded.
doctrine_prediction: A principled adherent of standing doctrine holds that Congress may create new legal rights through statute, but it cannot define away Article III's injury-in-fact requirement simply by labeling a procedural violation as an injury. Courts must independently assess whether an alleged statutory violation is concrete — whether it actually harmed the plaintiff or presents a sufficient risk of real harm — rather than deferring to Congress's judgment that any violation causes cognizable injury.
r_party_prediction: Republicans and business groups supported strict standing limits that would require concrete harm for FCRA class actions, reducing class-action litigation exposure for companies based on technical statutory violations that caused no real-world damage.
d_party_prediction: Democrats and consumer advocates supported broad standing to enforce consumer protection statutes, arguing Congress's decision to create a private right of action should be respected and that statutory violations themselves cause legally cognizable harm.
signal_explanation: Kagan joined the majority alongside the four R-appointed justices, providing a doctrine-over-party signal. Ginsburg and Sotomayor dissented. The cross-partisan character of the 6-2 vote moderates the diagnostic signal, as does the procedural posture — the Court remanded rather than deciding standing definitively.

Thomas's concurrence substantially limited the majority's reach by emphasizing that historical common-law analogues can establish concrete injury, which many FCRA plaintiffs could satisfy on remand.

Kagan's joining the majority may reflect her genuine view about Article III limits rather than a departure from partisan preference, since she has been consistent about requiring concrete injury in subsequent cases.

title: TransUnion LLC v. Ramirez
description: The Court held 5-4 that most members of a class action against TransUnion lacked standing under the FCRA because their inaccurate credit reports had never been sent to third parties — meaning they suffered no concrete injury despite the technical statutory violation. Thomas dissented, breaking from the conservative bloc.
factual_background: TransUnion erroneously flagged many customers as potential terrorists and drug traffickers due to a flawed OFAC-alert system. Only a small subset of class members had their inaccurate reports actually disseminated to third-party businesses; the majority had inaccurate data in their files that was never sent out. The class certified under FCRA sought statutory damages for all members regardless of dissemination.
doctrine_prediction: A principled adherent of standing doctrine holds that concrete injury requires a real-world impact traceable to the defendant's conduct, not merely the existence of inaccurate records in a file that no third party has seen. Class members whose inaccurate reports were never disseminated had no more than a potential future harm and cannot satisfy Article III without showing their statutory rights were violated in a way that caused them harm analogous to a common-law injury.
r_party_prediction: Republicans and business groups supported limiting class-action standing to plaintiffs with concrete real-world harm, reducing large FCRA class-action liability for companies based on technical violations that caused no demonstrated real harm.
d_party_prediction: Democrats and consumer advocates argued Congress's decision to create FCRA rights should be respected and that holding inaccurate, defamatory data — even unsent — is itself a legally cognizable harm consistent with common-law defamation analogues.
signal_explanation: Thomas's notable dissent breaking from the conservative majority is highly diagnostic. He argued that Congress can create enforceable legal rights that are themselves concrete injuries — a position that aligned with the D-appointed dissenters but contradicted the R-appointed majority. The 5-4 split otherwise follows party lines with Thomas as the outlier.

title: Department of Education v. Brown
description: The Court unanimously held that two student-loan borrowers who were not eligible for a particular forgiveness program lacked standing to challenge that program. factual_background: The Biden administration's student-loan forgiveness plan forgave up to $10,000 in federal student loan debt for eligible borrowers. Two plaintiffs — who held loans that made them ineligible for the specific forgiveness program they challenged — argued the program was unlawfully under-inclusive and that the HEROES Act did not authorize it. The Court reached the standing question without addressing the merits.
doctrine_prediction: A principled adherent of standing doctrine holds that plaintiffs must challenge a government action that actually injures them, not challenge favorable government programs for being insufficiently generous to them. A plaintiff who seeks a government benefit and is denied it under program criteria challenges the exclusion, not the program's existence — and challenging the exclusion of others from a competing program does not establish the required causal connection between the challenged action and the plaintiff's injury.

Nebraska); these particular plaintiffs were attempting to challenge the program from an unusual angle. R-party position generally supported strict standing limits but would have preferred a merits ruling striking down the plan.

signal_explanation: The unanimous decision limits its diagnostic utility — all nine justices agreed these plaintiffs lacked standing. However, the doctrinal alignment is meaningful: R-appointed justices who voted for strict standing here also voted against the forgiveness plan's merits in other cases (Biden v. Nebraska), suggesting doctrine and party interest both pointed to the same outcome for D-appointed justices and a more complex alignment for R-appointed justices.

title: Arizona v. United States
description: The Court held 5-3 that three provisions of Arizona's SB 1070 immigration enforcement law were preempted by federal law, while unanimously allowing enforcement of Section 2(B) requiring officers to check immigration status during lawful stops. Kennedy wrote for the majority.
factual_background: Arizona enacted SB 1070 in 2010, which created state-law immigration enforcement mechanisms including criminal penalties for unlawfully present aliens and authorization for police to arrest persons on immigration grounds. The federal government sued Arizona arguing federal immigration law preempted the state scheme. The Supreme Court struck three provisions but preserved the 'show me your papers' status-check requirement.
doctrine_prediction: A principled adherent of federalism doctrine holds that immigration is a domain where federal supremacy under the Supremacy Clause is strongest because it involves foreign relations and national uniformity. However, states retain police power authority to regulate conduct within their borders, and not every state law touching immigration is preempted. The preemption analysis must carefully distinguish between state laws that conflict with federal objectives and state laws that merely supplement federal enforcement.

Arizona's Republican governor and legislature passed SB 1070 as a response to what they characterized as federal failure to secure the border.

The 5-3 lineup (Kennedy writing, joined by Roberts, Ginsburg, Breyer, Sotomayor) diverged from the party-line expectation that R-appointed justices would uphold Arizona's law. Thomas dissented along with Alito (partially), maintaining the full R-party position.

title: Shelby County v. Holder
description: The Court held 5-4 that Section 4(b) of the Voting Rights Act — the coverage formula determining which jurisdictions must seek federal preclearance before changing voting laws — was unconstitutional because it relied on outdated 1965 data and violated the equal-sovereignty principle among states.
factual_background: The Voting Rights Act of 1965 required jurisdictions with a history of voting discrimination to obtain federal preclearance before changing election laws. Congress reauthorized the Act in 2006 using the same coverage formula from 1965. Shelby County, Alabama argued the formula was no longer justified by current conditions. The Court struck down the formula without addressing Section 5 preclearance directly.
doctrine_prediction: A principled adherent of federalism doctrine holds that Congress's power to enforce constitutional amendments does not permit it to impose disparate burdens on states indefinitely based on historical conduct without updating the justification. States have equal sovereignty and the federal government's intrusive oversight of state election administration must be grounded in current evidence of constitutional violations, not conditions that may have changed substantially over 50 years.
r_party_prediction: Republicans broadly supported striking down the VRA coverage formula as an outdated imposition on Southern states and an infringement on state sovereignty in managing their own elections; the R-party had long criticized the preclearance regime as unnecessary and discriminatory against covered states.
d_party_prediction: Democrats strongly defended the VRA preclearance requirement as essential for protecting minority voting rights and argued that Congress had adequate evidence of continued voting discrimination to justify reauthorization; the D-party treated the VRA as a foundational civil rights protection.
signal_explanation: The 5-4 split precisely follows partisan appointment lines on one of the most consequential voting-rights decisions in decades. Roberts writing for the five R-appointed justices and Ginsburg writing for the four D-appointed dissenters is a maximally partisan alignment pattern on a high-salience case.

title: Bond v. United States

factual_background: Carol Bond poisoned her husband's lover with toxic chemicals she obtained from her employer and ordered online. Federal prosecutors charged her under the Chemical Weapons Convention Implementation Act rather than state law. The Court avoided the constitutional question by narrowly construing the statute.

The power to make treaties does not give Congress unlimited authority to federalize all crimes involving means covered by a treaty; the federalism canon of avoiding unnecessary constitutional questions in treaty implementation preserves the states' traditional role in prosecuting local crimes.

d_party_prediction: The Obama administration defended the broad application of the Chemical Weapons Convention Implementation Act, arguing that treaty obligations required comprehensive federal enforcement authority and that Congress had authority to implement the CWC broadly.

The outcome was cross-partisan (Roberts joined liberal justices while Thomas, Scalia, and Alito wrote separately to reach the constitutional question), reducing diagnostic value.

D-appointed justices (seats 4, 5) are coded party-partial because they agreed with the limiting outcome but on narrower statutory grounds, not on federalism principle.
We use 2009–2017 period party positions.
counterarguments: The cross-partisan majority (Roberts joining liberal justices) on the statutory question makes it difficult to characterize the decision as partisan.
Thomas, Scalia, and Alito reaching the constitutional question may be more doctrinally principled than Roberts's avoidance approach, but it is also more outcome-determinative.
D-appointed justices joining the majority outcome (if not the reasoning) suggests the 'avoid the constitutional question' approach can produce cross-partisan results without being truly diagnostic.

title: Murphy v. NCAA
description: The Court held 6-3 that the Professional and Amateur Sports Protection Act (PASPA) — which prohibited states from authorizing sports gambling — violated the anticommandeering doctrine by commanding states not to enact their own laws. Alito wrote for the majority. Kagan joined the majority, breaking from the expected Democratic alignment.
factual_background: New Jersey sought to legalize sports betting at casinos and racetracks. PASPA prohibited states from 'authorizing by law' sports gambling. New Jersey argued the federal law unconstitutionally commandeered state legislatures by prohibiting them from repealing their own anti-gambling laws. The Court extended the anticommandeering principle to prohibitions on state legislation, not just affirmative mandates.
doctrine_prediction: A principled adherent of federalism doctrine holds that the anticommandeering principle protects state sovereignty by preventing Congress from directing the legislative processes of the states. This principle applies equally when Congress prohibits states from enacting laws as when it commands them to enact laws; in either case, the federal government is dictating the content of state legislation, which violates the constitutional structure of dual sovereignty.
r_party_prediction: Republicans and federalism advocates supported the anticommandeering ruling, consistent with the R-party's traditional support for state authority and opposition to federal mandates on state legislative processes.
d_party_prediction: The Obama-era PASPA was enacted with bipartisan support and the D-party institutional position was to defend federal authority over the sports gambling domain; the Democratic position was that PASPA was a valid exercise of Congress's commerce power.
signal_explanation: Kagan joining the majority against the expected D-party position of defending federal authority over state legislation provides a strong doctrine-over-party signal. The 6-3 alignment includes two D-appointed justices (Kagan and partially Ginsburg) supporting the federalism holding, while only Sotomayor and Breyer clearly dissented.

seat_1.vote: Joined the majority striking down PASPA; concurred separately to argue the anticommandeering principle flows directly from the Constitution's structure and is essential to preserve state sovereignty

seat_2.vote: Joined the majority striking down PASPA as violating the anticommandeering doctrine

seat_3.vote: Wrote the majority opinion holding that PASPA's prohibition on state authorization of sports gambling commandeered state legislatures in violation of the Tenth Amendment

seat_4.vote: Dissented in part; agreed PASPA's authorization prohibition was invalid but would have preserved the portion of PASPA prohibiting states from licensing betting operations through their own governmental entities

seat_5.vote: Joined the majority on the anticommandeering holding; wrote separately to note that the Court had correctly extended New York v. United States to cover prohibitory commandeering as well as affirmative commandeering

seat_6.vote: Joined the majority striking down PASPA as an unconstitutional commandeering of state legislative authority

assumptions: Seat 7 (Kavanaugh) omitted: confirmed October 2018; Murphy was decided May 14, 2018.
Seats 8–9 omitted: not yet on the Court.
Kagan (seat_5) is coded doctrine-yes/party-no because she joined the anticommandeering holding against the expected D-party position of defending federal authority over state gambling law.
Sotomayor (seat_4) is coded partial/partial: she agreed on the main anticommandeering holding but dissented from severing the entire statute.
We use 2017–2021 period party positions.
counterarguments: PASPA was enacted in 1992 with broad bipartisan support, meaning the D-party position on this specific statute may not have been as clearly defined as the coding suggests.
The sports gambling industry's strong lobbying interest in the outcome may have influenced the political alignment of the case in ways that cross traditional partisan lines.

Kagan's joining the majority may reflect her genuine commitment to federalism doctrine rather than a departure from partisan preference, since she had previously supported anticommandeering principles in academic writings.

title: Haaland v. Brackeen
description: The Court held 7-2 that the Indian Child Welfare Act's preferences for placing Native American children with Native families in adoption proceedings did not violate the anticommandeering doctrine or equal protection. Barrett wrote the majority. Thomas dissented.
factual_background: Non-Native American foster parents and several states challenged ICWA, which requires states in child welfare proceedings involving Native American children to preference placement with Native families or tribal members, and to notify tribes of proceedings. Challengers argued ICWA unconstitutionally commandeered state family law processes and made race-based placements.
doctrine_prediction: A principled adherent of federalism doctrine holds that while Congress may set federal standards that states must implement, it cannot commandeer state courts or agencies to administer a federal regulatory scheme as federal agents. The anticommandeering inquiry is whether the federal law merely sets minimum standards or instead directs state officials to implement federal policy using their sovereign institutional resources.
r_party_prediction: Texas and other R-led states challenged ICWA as an unconstitutional intrusion into state family law and an improper race-based classification, consistent with R-party opposition to race-conscious federal programs and support for limiting federal mandates on states.
d_party_prediction: Democrats and tribal advocates supported ICWA as a valid exercise of Congress's plenary authority over Indian affairs and a necessary protection for Native American families against the historical abuses of forced removal; the D-party position strongly supported the statute.
signal_explanation: The 7-2 decision with broad cross-partisan support — including Barrett writing the majority joined by Sotomayor, Kagan, Jackson, and also Kavanaugh and Roberts — limits diagnostic value. Only Thomas (and partly Gorsuch on specific provisions) dissented on the anticommandeering question.

seat_1.vote: Dissented; argued ICWA's placement preferences constituted racial classifications that violate equal protection and that the statutes impermissibly directed state officials to apply federal policy

seat_2.vote: Joined the majority upholding ICWA; the placement preferences reflected Congress's plenary authority over Indian affairs and the anticommandeering doctrine was not violated

seat_3.vote: Joined parts of the majority upholding ICWA's core placement preferences, though concurred separately on some provisions

seat_4.vote: Joined the majority upholding ICWA's placement preferences under Congress's Indian affairs power

seat_5.vote: Joined the majority upholding ICWA; Congress's plenary power over Indian affairs provided an independent basis for the statute apart from the Commerce Clause

seat_6.vote: Joined portions of the majority; wrote separately to note open questions about equal protection that the Court declined to resolve

seat_7.vote: Joined the majority upholding ICWA's core provisions

seat_8.vote: Wrote the majority opinion upholding ICWA; held the statute did not violate anticommandeering, equal protection, or the nondelegation doctrine

seat_9.vote: Joined the majority upholding ICWA's placement preferences and tribal notification requirements

assumptions: Signal level is low: the 7-2 vote with broad cross-partisan support limits diagnostic value.
Indian affairs law occupies a unique constitutional space (Congress's plenary power doctrine) that does not map cleanly onto typical federalism analysis.
Roberts, Alito, Kavanaugh, and Barrett voting to uphold ICWA against the R-state challengers' position is coded party-no because R-led states brought the challenge.
We use 2021–2025 period party positions.
counterarguments: Congress's plenary authority over Indian affairs is an independent constitutional basis distinct from federalism doctrine, making ICWA's validity a sui generis question not squarely governed by anticommandeering precedent.
The 7-2 vote may reflect a genuine cross-partisan consensus that Indian affairs law occupies a special constitutional category rather than partisan alignment.
Thomas's dissent is the only vote clearly tracking the R-state challengers' position; all other R-appointed justices rejected the challenge, which complicates any partisan framing.

title: Moore v. Harper
description: The Court held 6-3 that the Elections Clause does not immunize state legislatures from review by state courts applying state constitutional law when they regulate federal elections. Roberts wrote for the majority, rejecting the 'independent state legislature' theory and holding state courts can check state legislative overreach in federal election administration.
factual_background: North Carolina Republicans drew a congressional map that the North Carolina Supreme Court struck down as an extreme partisan gerrymander under the state constitution. The Republican-controlled legislature petitioned the U.S. Supreme Court, arguing that the Elections Clause gives state legislatures plenary, unreviewable authority over federal elections — the 'independent state legislature' theory. The U.S. Supreme Court rejected this theory.
doctrine_prediction: A principled adherent of federalism doctrine holds that the Elections Clause, in granting state 'Legislatures' authority to regulate federal elections, does not exempt those legislatures from the constraints of their own state constitutions as interpreted by state courts. States operate as complete constitutional systems, and treating legislatures as independent from state constitutions would fracture state sovereignty by elevating one branch of state government above the state's own foundational law.
r_party_prediction: Republican-controlled legislatures in gerrymandering states sought a ruling that state courts cannot override their federal election maps under state law, which would have significantly expanded R-controlled legislatures' power over congressional districts by removing state judicial oversight.
d_party_prediction: Democrats strongly opposed the independent state legislature theory as a vehicle for locking in partisan gerrymanders and potentially disrupting state court oversight of federal election administration more broadly; the D-party position supported state constitutional checks on legislative authority over elections.
signal_explanation: Roberts writing the majority and joining D-appointed justices against the position of Republican-controlled legislatures is diagnostic. Kavanaugh and Barrett also joined, meaning three R-appointed justices voted against the R-party's position. Alito, Thomas, and Gorsuch dissented on mootness grounds (they argued the case was moot after the NC Supreme Court changed its composition), limiting the signal from the dissent.

seat_1.vote: Dissented; argued the case was moot after North Carolina's new Supreme Court reversed course on the gerrymander and that the majority should not have reached the merits of the independent-state-legislature theory

seat_2.vote: Wrote the majority opinion rejecting the independent state legislature theory; held state courts retain authority to review state legislative action in federal elections under state constitutional law

seat_3.vote: Dissented; argued the case was moot and the majority was issuing an advisory opinion by reaching the merits after the state court had reversed its own prior ruling

seat_4.vote: Joined the majority rejecting the independent state legislature theory and affirming state court oversight of federal elections under state constitutional law

seat_5.vote: Joined the majority; argued that accepting the independent state legislature theory would have destabilized election administration nationwide

seat_6.vote: Dissented; argued the case was moot after North Carolina's Supreme Court reversed course, and that the majority issued an advisory opinion by reaching the ISL question

seat_7.vote: Joined the majority rejecting the independent state legislature theory

seat_8.vote: Joined the majority rejecting the independent state legislature theory and upholding state court oversight of federal elections

seat_9.vote: Joined the majority rejecting the independent state legislature theory

assumptions: We use 2021–2025 period party positions: R = supported ISL theory favoring R-controlled legislatures; D = opposed ISL theory.

Thomas, Alito, Gorsuch are coded partial/party-yes because their dissents focused on mootness rather than endorsing ISL on the merits, but their mootness position would have prevented a ruling against the R-party's preferred theory.
Roberts, Kavanaugh, and Barrett are coded doctrine-yes/party-no because they rejected ISL against the position of R-controlled legislatures.
The mootness basis of the three dissents is a complication: the dissenters may have believed the case was genuinely moot, not that ISL was correct on the merits.
counterarguments: The three dissenters' mootness argument has genuine legal merit — after the NC Supreme Court reversed itself, it was not clear there was a live controversy for the U.S. Supreme Court to decide.
Roberts's majority opinion could be read as strengthening state sovereignty overall (state constitutions bind state legislatures) rather than as a partisan outcome favoring Democrats.
Kavanaugh and Barrett joining the majority against ISL may reflect genuine doctrinal views about state constitutional law rather than departure from partisan preference.

title: Agency for Int'l Dev. v. Alliance for Open Society Int'l
description: The Court held 6-2 that a federal law conditioning AIDS-relief funding on recipients having an explicit organizational policy opposing prostitution and sex trafficking violated the First Amendment by compelling speech beyond the government program's scope. Roberts wrote the majority.
factual_background: Congress conditioned receipt of federal AIDS-relief funds on recipient organizations having 'a policy explicitly opposing prostitution and sex trafficking.' U.S.-based affiliates of international health organizations argued the requirement violated their First Amendment rights by mandating adoption of a specific viewpoint as a condition of federal funding. The government argued the condition merely restricted how federal money could be spent.
doctrine_prediction: A principled adherent of spending-authority doctrine holds that Congress may attach conditions to federal grants that require recipients to spend money in ways consistent with the program's purposes, but may not use funding conditions to compel organizations to adopt specific ideological positions that extend beyond the federally funded program. The distinction between restricting the use of federal funds (permissible) and compelling a recipient to adopt an organization-wide viewpoint (unconstitutional) is essential to protecting First Amendment freedoms from funding leverage.
r_party_prediction: Congressional Republicans broadly supported the anti-prostitution pledge requirement as a moral condition on foreign aid, consistent with R-party positions on sex trafficking and opposition to normalizing prostitution. The R-party had championed this requirement in the original legislation.
d_party_prediction: The Obama administration defended the pledge requirement as a permissible condition limiting how federal funds could be spent and who could receive them; however, many D-aligned civil liberties organizations supported striking it down as compelled speech that undermined public health organizations' effectiveness abroad.
signal_explanation: Roberts writing the majority striking down an R-favored congressional policy is a strong doctrine-over-party signal. Thomas dissenting alongside Scalia to uphold the pledge requirement maintains the R-partisan position. The D-party position was itself divided — the Obama administration defended the pledge — making the D-appointed justices' votes partially diagnostic.

seat_1.vote: Dissented; argued the anti-prostitution pledge requirement was a permissible restriction on how federal funds could be spent and who could be eligible to receive them, not a compulsion of private speech

seat_2.vote: Wrote the majority opinion striking down the pledge requirement as compelled speech that went beyond restricting how federal funds were spent to mandating organizations adopt an organization-wide ideological policy

seat_3.vote: Joined Thomas's dissent; argued the government may require recipients to agree with the conditions of its funding program as a prerequisite for receiving federal money

seat_4.vote: Joined the majority striking down the pledge requirement as an unconstitutional condition on the receipt of federal funds

seat_5.vote: Joined the majority striking down the pledge requirement; argued the government may not use federal funding to compel private organizations to endorse government policy as their own

assumptions: Seats 6–9 omitted: not yet on the Court.

D-appointed justices (seats 4, 5) are coded party-partial because the Obama administration (a D administration) defended the pledge requirement, creating a split between the executive branch and D-aligned civil liberties groups.
Roberts (seat_2) is coded party-no because he struck down an R-sponsored policy condition against the R-party's stated political position.
We use 2009–2017 period party positions.
counterarguments: The case is as much a First Amendment case as a spending-power case; coding it primarily as a spending-authority case may understate the free-speech dimension.
The Obama administration defending the R-designed pledge condition makes the partisan alignment complex — the executive branch's position crossed party lines.
Thomas and Scalia's dissent that funding conditions may require ideological agreement is a defensible view of the unconstitutional conditions doctrine, not necessarily a partisan position.

title: Trump v. Vance
description: The Court held 7-2 that a sitting President is not absolutely immune from a state grand jury's subpoena for personal financial records; the state grand jury's subpoena to Trump's accounting firm was enforceable. Roberts wrote for the majority.
factual_background: Manhattan District Attorney Cy Vance subpoenaed Trump's long-time accounting firm Mazars USA for financial records as part of a grand jury investigation. Trump argued a sitting President has absolute immunity from state criminal process. The Court rejected absolute immunity but remanded for lower courts to consider whether the specific subpoena posed heightened burdens on presidential functions.
doctrine_prediction: A principled adherent of presidential-immunity doctrine holds that the Constitution does not provide a sitting President with absolute immunity from state criminal process. Presidential accountability to the law — including state criminal investigations — is a foundational constitutional principle. While a President may seek to show that a specific subpoena poses undue burden on executive functions, there is no categorical bar to state criminal process applicable to presidents.
r_party_prediction: The Trump administration and Republicans argued for broad presidential immunity from state criminal process, asserting that allowing states to investigate sitting presidents would create harassment risks and distract from presidential duties; the R-party's institutional interest was in protecting Trump from the Manhattan DA's investigation.
d_party_prediction: Democrats and the Manhattan DA's office supported enforcing the subpoena, arguing no person — including the President — is above the law and that state criminal investigations must be able to proceed regardless of the subject's office.
signal_explanation: Gorsuch and Kavanaugh joining the seven-justice majority against the Trump administration's position is highly diagnostic — two Trump-appointed justices voted against their appointer's claimed immunity. Thomas and Alito dissented, partially maintaining the pro-immunity position. Roberts writing the majority against the R-party's interest provides the strongest doctrine-over-party signal.

seat_1.vote: Dissented in part; would have imposed additional judicial safeguards before any state criminal subpoena for presidential records could be enforced, arguing the majority's standard was insufficient to protect against harassment of sitting presidents

seat_2.vote: Wrote the majority opinion holding there is no absolute presidential immunity from state criminal subpoenas; the President may challenge specific subpoenas on undue-burden grounds but cannot categorically block state grand jury process

seat_3.vote: Dissented; argued the majority's framework was insufficiently protective of the presidency from politically motivated state prosecutorial harassment

seat_4.vote: Joined the majority holding that no person, including the President, is absolutely immune from state grand jury subpoenas

seat_5.vote: Joined the majority; argued the majority's holding correctly applied the principle that the President is subject to the law

seat_6.vote: Joined the majority holding that absolute presidential immunity from state criminal process is not supported by the Constitution's text, history, or precedent

seat_7.vote: Joined the majority rejecting absolute presidential immunity from state grand jury subpoenas

assumptions: Seat 8 (Barrett) omitted: confirmed October 2020; Trump v. Vance was decided July 9, 2020.
Seat 9 (Jackson) omitted: not yet on the Court.
Gorsuch (seat_6) and Kavanaugh (seat_7) are coded doctrine-yes/party-no: they are Trump-appointed justices who voted against Trump's claimed immunity, the paradigmatic doctrine-over-party signal.
We use 2017–2021 period party positions: R = 'opposed' to executive restraint (supporting broad presidential immunity for Trump); D = 'aligned' (supporting criminal accountability of presidents).
counterarguments: Roberts's majority actually remanded for lower courts to consider whether the subpoena imposed undue burdens — meaning Trump could still challenge the subpoena's specific terms, limiting the practical effect of the ruling.

Thomas and Alito's dissents are framed as institutional concerns about the risk of politically motivated state prosecutorial harassment, which is a principled institutional argument rather than a purely partisan defense of Trump.
The 7-2 majority including all three Trump-appointed justices who were on the Court at the time provides unusually strong evidence against the partisan-capture thesis for Gorsuch and Kavanaugh.

title: Trump v. Mazars USA, LLP
description: The Court held 7-2 that congressional committee subpoenas for a sitting President's personal financial records are subject to heightened scrutiny and must be justified by a legitimate legislative purpose proportionate to the intrusion on the Executive. Roberts wrote the majority; the case was remanded for lower-court application of this standard.
factual_background: Three House committees subpoenaed Trump's accounting firm and banks for personal financial records, claiming legislative purposes including ethics legislation and national security concerns. Trump sued to block the subpoenas. The D.C. Circuit and Second Circuit upheld the subpoenas; the Supreme Court vacated and remanded with a new standard requiring courts to evaluate whether the subpoenas served a valid legislative need, were appropriately tailored, and were not motivated by exposure rather than legislation.
doctrine_prediction: A principled adherent of presidential-immunity doctrine holds that the legislative branch's subpoena power does not extend to unlimited demands for presidential financial records absent a demonstrated nexus to a legitimate legislative function. While no person is entirely above legislative process, a sitting President's personal records demand heightened scrutiny because unchecked congressional subpoena power could paralyze the executive through perpetual legislative investigation. Courts must independently balance the legislative need against executive-branch interests.
r_party_prediction: The Trump administration and Republicans argued Congress had no legislative basis for the financial records subpoenas and that the subpoenas were designed to embarrass and harass a sitting president rather than advance legislation; the R-party's institutional interest was in blocking the subpoenas entirely.
d_party_prediction: House Democrats and the D-party sought to enforce the subpoenas as part of oversight investigations into whether legislation was needed on financial ethics, presidential conflicts of interest, and foreign financial entanglements; the D-party position was that Congress's broad oversight authority supported enforcement.
signal_explanation: The 7-2 decision against the Trump administration — with both Gorsuch and Kavanaugh in the majority — provides strong doctrine-over-party signal for those two justices. Roberts imposing a heightened-scrutiny standard rather than blocking the subpoenas entirely also falls short of the maximum R-party interest in full protection. Thomas and Alito dissenting and arguing for stronger protection for the President aligns with R-party interest.

seat_1.vote: Dissented; argued the majority's heightened-scrutiny standard was insufficient and that congressional subpoenas for presidential financial records should require a clear statement of specific legislative need before courts enforce them

seat_2.vote: Wrote the majority opinion imposing heightened scrutiny on congressional subpoenas for presidential financial records; remanded for lower courts to apply the new standard balancing legislative need against executive-branch interests

seat_3.vote: Dissented; argued Congress must affirmatively demonstrate a specific, legitimate legislative purpose before any court should enforce a subpoena for presidential personal records
